file_name: SightLife Surgical, Inc. - STRATEGIC SALES _ MARKETING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Surgical represents and warrants that as of the Effective Date, there is no conflict of interest which would prevent Surgical from performing the Services for Imprimis, and that Surgical is not under any legal or contractual relationship with any third party which is inconsistent with any provision of this Agreement. During the Term of this Agreement, Surgical will not to enter into any other agreement or arrangement that will directly or indirectly compete with the Services to be rendered hereunder, as such agreement shall be considered a breach of this Agreement. In the event that Surgical becomes aware of any potential or actual conflicts of interest regarding the provision of the Services, Surgical shall promptly disclose the fact and nature of such conflict to Imprimis.
perturbation.changed_text: Surgical represents and warrants that as of the Effective Date, there is no conflict of interest which would prevent Surgical from performing the Services for Imprimis. During the Term of this Agreement, Surgical will not to enter into any other agreement or arrangement that will directly or indirectly compete with the Services to be rendered hereunder, as such agreement shall be considered a breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'In the event that Surgical becomes aware of any potential or actual conflicts of interest regarding the provision of the Services, Surgical shall promptly disclose the fact and nature of such conflict to Imprimis' eliminates a key element of transparency and good faith. This omission creates a situation where Surgical is not obligated to disclose conflicts of interest as they arise. This introduces legal risks related to fiduciary duties and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 17200 prohibits unfair competition, which includes unfair or fraudulent business acts or practices. By removing the obligation to disclose potential conflicts of interest, the contract creates a situation where Surgical could potentially engage in practices that, while not explicitly illegal, are unfair and detrimental to Imprimis. The lack of a disclosure requirement enables Surgical to prioritize its own interests (or those of another client) over Imprimis's, which may constitute an unfair business practice under California law.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 5. Enforcement [17200 - 17210] ( Chapter 5 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 299. ) 17200. As used in this chapter, unfair competition shall mean and include any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice and unfair, deceptive, untrue or misleading advertising and any act prohibited by Chapter 1 (commencing with Section 17500) of Part 3 of Division 7 of the Business and Professions Code. (Amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 430, Sec. 2. Effective January 1, 1993.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract text includes a clause that requires Surgical to disclose any potential or actual conflicts of interest to Imprimis. The changed text removes this obligation. The Law Explanation states that this omission could enable Surgical to prioritize its own interests over Imprimis's, potentially constituting an unfair business practice. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines unfair competition under California Business and Professions Code Section 17200 as 'any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice'. By removing the disclosure requirement, the changed text creates a situation where Surgical could engage in practices that are unfair to Imprimis, thus contradicting the law's prohibition of unfair business practices. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' where removing essential information creates legal loopholes, making the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: The Sales Team shall use only such marketing and other materials for the Product in the Territory as are expressly approved in writing in advance by Imprimis, are consistent in all respects with the external marketing of such Product by Imprimis, include all warnings and instructions applicable for the proper use of the Product, comply with all applicable laws and regulations in the Territory, and do not contain any claims regarding a Product or its performance that the Sales Team does not reasonably demonstrate are supported by such Product or its performance.
perturbation.changed_text: The Sales Team shall use marketing and other materials for the Product in the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: The original text includes a clause requiring that marketing materials 'include all warnings and instructions applicable for the proper use of the Product'. Removing this clause means the Sales Team is not contractually obligated to use materials with proper warnings and instructions which introduces the risk of the Sales Team using non-compliant materials, leading to potential liability issues for Imprimis.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 352 - Misbranded drugs and devices
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S.C. § 352 defines misbranding of drugs and devices. One way a drug or device can be misbranded is if its labeling (including marketing materials) lacks adequate warnings. By removing the contractual requirement for the Sales Team to use marketing materials with all applicable warnings and instructions, the revised text creates a significant risk that the Sales Team will use materials that result in the Product being misbranded, thus violating federal law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit requirement for the Sales Team to include 'all warnings and instructions applicable for the proper use of the Product' in their marketing materials. The Law Explanation states this removal creates a risk of misbranding under 21 U.S.C. § 352, as the product's labeling (including marketing materials) may lack adequate warnings. While the scraped law snippet is incomplete, the Law Explanation's mention of misbranding due to inadequate warnings, combined with the Perturbation Explanation highlighting potential liability issues due to non-compliant materials, supports the existence of a contradiction. The original text ensured compliance by explicitly requiring warnings, while the changed text opens the door to non-compliance, directly conflicting with the intent to prevent misbranding.
